messages: I'm really trying to learn to cook better so weeknight dinners are healthier and less stressful. I usually only have about 45 minutes in the evening to prep and cook.
My kitchen is tiny — I have a two-burner gas stove and only one 10-inch cast-iron skillet; I don't have an oven or many extra pans.
We mostly eat vegetarian food at home, though I sometimes prepare fish for my partner; I generally cook portions for two people.
My main problems: vegetables often steam instead of getting a nice sear when the pan gets crowded, and I tend to under-season when I try to keep things light.
Quick random question: what's the average lifespan of a worker honeybee?
Also, does anyone have a favorite lightweight daypack brand? I'm thinking about replacing my old commuter bag.
I need a short sci-fi read for a weekend trip — any suggestions that are engaging and not 700 pages?
Fun fact check: which country has the most UNESCO World Heritage sites? I always mix that up with the country that has the most islands.
I've been meaning to learn a few basic phrases in Japanese before a potential trip — what's a super useful greeting to start with?
Is there a simple trick to fold a fitted sheet that actually looks neat? My linen closet is chaos.
For movie night: should I pick a light romantic comedy or a tense thriller? I'm indecisive.
Tiny curiosity: how long does it actually take for a plastic container to fully decompose if it's littered outdoors?
I signed up for an evening pottery class as a hobby — anyone know what to expect the first week?
Is it better to stretch a 5K training plan over eight weeks or cram it into six if you have limited time?
I spilled red wine on my friend's couch last month — what's the fastest household trick for removing a fresh wine stain?
Also, I heard a weird theory that octopuses are aliens. Is there any scientific basis, or is that just a meme?

Okay, back to dinner — if I only have a single heavy skillet and limited stovetop space on a typical weekday evening, what sequencing or tiny hacks help me get vegetables crisp instead of ending up soggy?
When I'm trying to keep salt down for health reasons, what concentrated flavor boosters or techniques can make a dish taste fuller without reaching for extra sodium?
Also, given the short evening window I usually have, how could I time a plant-forward main and the occasional small seafood side so both finish around the same time?
Finally, any practical ways to stretch the capacity of a small skillet so a double portion doesn't crowd the pan and just steam rather than brown?
